setup_simulation_howto.expected_output: To start a simulation workflow go to Workflows > Category: Simulation. Then choose a scale. After this, you will be prompted to select a model for simulation (depending on the scale you chose). You can then configure your simulation. First in Initialize, give it a Name and Description. You can then add different types of Stimuli and Recordings on the left hand side. You can change parameters of the stimuli or the target of the stimulus by creating a new Neuron Set and selcting it as the Neuron Set target of the stimulus. You can additionally make  modifications to the model (neuronal and synaptic modifications). This will generate the simulations in your campaign. You can then select simulations to launch.
setup_simulation_howto.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for setting up simulations now.## How to set up a simulation on the Open Brain Platform

Below is a concise, step‑by‑step guide that follows the platform workflows described in the documentation (Workflows + “Designing a Simulation” + beta configuration guides). Follow these steps in the web UI to design, generate, and launch simulations.

---

## 1) Open the Simulate workflow
- Go to your project and open the Workflows tab.
- Click the Simulate category card. A second row of simulation Type cards appears (Single neuron, Synaptome, Paired neurons (beta), Small microcircuit (beta), etc.).
- Click the Type card you want (eg. Paired neurons (beta) or Small microcircuit (beta)). This opens a model picker scoped to that simulation type.

Tips:
- The Workflows tab is the single entry point for starting and browsing simulation campaigns.
- The Type row only starts the draft workflow — no compute is used yet.

---

## 2) Choose a model (Model picker)
- The model picker lists models valid for the chosen Type and has Public / Project tabs.
- Use Search and Filters (brain region, name, etc.) to narrow results.
- Click a row to open the preview panel on the right (morphology, connectivity / thumbnails, metadata).
- When a preview matches, click Use model to enter the Configuration workspace. Optionally click View details to inspect the full entity page first.

Important pick advice:
- Prefer models with working thumbnails / previews; broken thumbnails may indicate incomplete models that can cause generation failures.
- For circuit-scale models you’ll see circuit-level metadata (neurons, synapses, connections).

---

## 3) Configuration workspace — overview
Layout:
- Top tabs: Configuration (form) and Simulations (generated artifacts).
- Three columns:
  - Left: Section navigator (Setup, Stimuli & Recordings, Circuit components / Manipulations, Events). Shows status icons (green check / yellow warning).
  - Middle: Section forms and editors for the currently selected section.
  - Right: Live preview of the selected model (3D / straight views, thumbnails, connectivity).

Sections (common across beta flows):
- Setup
  - Info (Campaign name and Campaign description) — both required
  - Initialization (duration, dt, extracellular Ca, v_init, random seed)
- Stimuli & Recordings
  - Stimuli — add one or more stimulus blocks
  - Recordings — define somatic voltage or other recordings
- Circuit components (only for circuit types)
  - Neuron sets — define groups (All Biophysical Neurons is present by default)
- Manipulations
  - Synaptic manipulations for circuit types; Neuronal manipulations for single-cell types
- Events
  - Timestamps (named markers) for synchronizing stimuli

Section status semantics:
- Yellow warning — required fields missing; blocks generation.
- Green check — section validated.
- Grey/empty — optional and nothing configured.

Quick rule:
- The Generate simulation(s) button is enabled only when **all required sections** show green checks.

---

## 4) Fill required fields (Setup → Info + Initialization)
- Info (required):
  - Campaign name — unique, human‑readable
  - Campaign description — what you are testing / why
  Both must be filled to unblock Generate.
- Initialization:
  - Duration (ms), Extracellular Ca (mM), Initial voltage (mV), Random seed, etc.
  - Defaults are pre-populated and usually valid; change if needed.

Tip:
- Initialization defaults are written into simulation_config.json. If you want to change global simulation parameters later, adjust Initialization before generating.

---

## 5) Define stimuli and recordings
Stimuli:
- Click Stimuli → Add Stimulus → choose type.
- Beta flows expose many stimulus types:
  - Relative (amplitude as % of each cell’s threshold) — recommended for heterogeneous populations (Normally distributed relative, Constant relative, Linear relative, Subthreshold relative, OU relative, etc.)
  - Absolute (amplitude in nA) — if you need absolute control
- Common fields: Duration (ms), Timestamps (events), Timestamp offset (ms), Neuron set (circuit types), Percentage of threshold or Amplitude.

Recordings:
- Click Recordings → Add Recording → choose recording type.
- Common: Soma voltage full experiment or time-window recordings; set dt / start / end as needed.

Tip:
- Use Relative stimuli by default so the same stimulus block works across heterogeneous neuron sets.
- Create named Timestamps (Events) if multiple stimuli must fire together — then choose those events in the Stimuli Timestamps dropdown.

---

## 6) Define neuron sets and manipulations (circuit models)
- Circuit components → Neuron sets: create custom neuron sets to target stimuli or recordings to subsets (for example, presynaptic neuron only).
- Manipulations:
  - Synaptic manipulations (circuit types) — scale conductances, disconnect pathways, change Mg/Ca, etc.
  - Neuronal manipulations (single-cell) — change ion-channel parameters in sections or whole neuron.

Note:
- Manipulations are optional and default to leaving the model as-is.

---

## 7) Parameter scans (sweeps)
- Numeric fields have a small plus-circle control. Click it to add multiple values (scan/grid).
- The campaign generated is the Cartesian product of all scanned numeric fields (one simulation per combination).
- While any required scan value is empty, that section shows a warning and Generate is disabled.

Tip:
- Scan sparingly until you verify the baseline — each additional scan value multiplies simulation count.

---

## 8) Generate the campaign
- When all required sections are green, click Generate simulation(s) at the bottom of the left column.
- The platform validates the configuration against the simulation schema.
- On success:
  - A campaign entity is created.
  - One Simulation object is created per scanned-parameter combination.
  - The UI switches to the Simulations tab where the generated simulations and their input JSON files are present.

What you’ll see on the Simulations tab:
- Left: simulation rows (Simulation 0 … N), selection checkboxes.
- Middle: the four input files (buttons) — circuit_config.json, node_sets.json, obi_one_coordinate.json, simulation_config.json.
- Right: JSON viewer for selected file.
- Footer: Launch simulations (N) button (green). Clicking it submits the selected simulations for compute (consumes credits).

Important:
- Campaign creation (“Done” in Activities) does not mean the simulations have run. Actual compute is only performed after you press Launch simulations (or equivalent action on the campaign page).

---

## 9) Inspect / copy the campaign artifacts
- Use the JSON viewer to inspect or copy:
  - circuit_config.json — SONATA circuit config
  - node_sets.json — named node sets
  - obi_one_coordinate.json — OBI-one scan coordinate metadata
  - simulation_config.json — SONATA simulation config (contains Initialization, stimuli/recordings if set)
- There’s a Copy simulation campaign ID button to copy the campaign UUID (useful for API / CLI use).

---

## 10) Launch, monitor, and retrieve results
- Select simulations on the Simulations tab and click Launch simulations to submit for compute.
- The Activities view (project home → Activities) is the audit trail showing campaign status (Created, Running, Done, Failed).
- After runs finish, you can download outputs from the campaign/simulation pages.

---

## 11) Failure modes & troubleshooting
- Pick models with working previews/thumbnail. Broken thumbnails are a leading indicator of model metadata problems and backend generation errors.
- If Generate silently fails (the UI stays on Configuration and Simulations stays inert), check browser console and Activities to see whether a campaign was partially persisted. Report the issue and consider re-picking the model.
- The platform validates configurations on Generate; fix any validation warnings before attempting to generate again.

---

## Quick cheat‑sheet (important anchors)
- Where to start: Project → Workflows → Simulate → pick Type → select model → Use model → Configuration tab
- Required to generate: Info (Campaign name + Campaign description) + any other required form fields (left-column status must be green)
- Generate → creates campaign + simulations (one per scanned combo)
- Launch → actually runs simulations (consumes credits)
- Activities → monitor campaigns and runs
- Copy campaign ID → useful for API/entitysdk interactions

---

## Practical tips (recap)
- Start from the model preview to confirm selection.
- Use Relative stimuli for heterogeneous cell sets.
- Define neuron sets before referencing them in stimuli/recordings.
- Create Timestamps (Events) first, then reference them from Stimuli.
- Scan sparingly at first; each extra parameter multiplies simulation count.
- Defaults often suffice for a smoke test — filling Info alone can create a one-simulation campaign.

---

If you'd like, I can:
- Walk you through creating a specific example campaign (tell me Type and model you want), or
- Validate a draft simulation configuration in your session state (I can read and edit the shared simulation config if you want to build it interactively). Which would you prefer?

submit_jobs_digital_brain.user: How can I submit jobs and get responses from the Digital Brain? What language or syntax can I use to communicate with it?
submit_jobs_digital_brain.expected_output: you submit simulation jobs through the Workflows > Simulate interface (graphical UI) or programmatically via Jupyter notebooks. The platform also has an AI chat assistant that accepts natural language queries for data exploration and literature search.
submit_jobs_digital_brain.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation about submitting simulations and interacting with the Digital Brain (simulation execution). I'll search the product docs for \simulation".## Short answer — how you submit jobs and get responses
- Use the Workflows → Simulate flow in the web app: pick a simulation Type, choose a model, fill the configuration panels, click **Generate simulation(s)** to create a simulation campaign, then click **Launch simulations** (or use the campaign actions) to submit compute.  
- After generation you get a campaign UUID you can copy; the campaign’s input files (SONATA / OBI JSON) are available and are the canonical spec the simulator executes. Use those JSON files or the campaign UUID if you need to run or access the campaign programmatically.

I'll summarize the UI steps, the file formats/syntax you can use to talk to the simulator, and how to retrieve results.

## Step-by-step (web UI)
1. Open Workflows in your project and click Simulate.  
2. Select the simulation Type (e.g., Single neuron, Paired neurons (beta), Small microcircuit (beta)).  
3. Pick a model from the model picker (Public / Project tabs). Open its preview and click **Use model**.  
4. Complete the Configuration tab (Info, Initialization, Stimuli, Recordings, Manipulations, Events, optional parameter scans). Sections show status icons — the campaign can be generated when all required sections are green.  
5. Click **Generate simulation(s)**. The platform validates and creates a Simulation Campaign and one or more Simulation entities (one per scanned-parameter combination).  
6. Switch to the Simulations tab, select simulation(s) and click **Launch simulations (N)** to submit compute. This is the action that consumes credits and starts execution.  
7. After runs finish you can view and download outputs from the activity/campaign pages.

## The language / syntax the Digital Brain accepts
- The simulator reads the generated campaign files — these are the authoritative program specification:
  - **circuit_config.json** — SONATA circuit config (networks.nodes, networks.edges, manifest, node files).  
  - **node_sets.json** — named node-set definitions used by stimuli/recordings.  
  - **obi_one_coordinate.json** — OBI-one campaign coordinates (scan axes).  
  - **simulation_config.json** — SONATA simulation config (initialization, inputs, reports, stimuli/recordings as serialized dicts).  
- In short: the runtime input is expressed as SONATA + OBI-one JSON artifacts. If you want to programmatically define or modify a job, produce valid SONATA/OBI JSON files matching these formats (the UI shows and lets you copy the exact JSON used).

Notes from the docs:
- The UI shows these four JSON files in the Simulations tab and provides a copy button / JSON viewer for each file. These JSON files are literally what the simulator consumes.
- Parameter scans in the UI produce a Cartesian product of numeric values; the resulting scan axes are encoded in obi_one_coordinate.json.

## Programmatic / CLI interaction (overview)
- The platform surfaces a stable campaign UUID after generation — use **Copy simulation campaign ID** (top-right) to get it. The docs note this UUID is the stable handle useful for API / entitysdk access or for reopening the campaign later.
- The generated JSON bundle (the four files above) is the programmatic contract you can reuse offline, edit, or submit via API or SDK (the docs specifically call out copying these JSONs out for offline use).
- The docs do not document a specific CLI command here, but they indicate the campaign UUID + the four JSON files are the primary artifacts you can use when interacting outside the web UI (API / SDK workflows).

## What you get back (responses / outputs)
- Simulation outputs are produced per SimulationResult (the platform stores run outputs). In the Simulations UI you can:
  - Monitor simulation status (Created → running → finished / failed).  
  - Download output artifacts after completion.  
  - View or copy the input JSON used for the run (for reproducibility).  
- Activities list (project Activities) provides an audit trail of campaigns and run status (Done / running / failed).

## Practical recommendations
- Start from the UI to build and validate a campaign; copy the four JSON files from the Simulations tab once generated — they are the exact runtime specification.  
- Use Relative stimulus types (UI default options) when targeting heterogeneous neuron sets — they scale to each cell’s threshold.  
- Scan numeric parameters sparingly — each additional value multiplies the number of generated simulations.  
- If you want to automate: generate the campaign once in the UI, copy the JSONs and the campaign UUID, then use the UUID + JSONs with API/SDK workflows (the docs point to the campaign UUID as the stable handle for programmatic access).

---

If you want, I can:
- Show an example minimal simulation_config.json produced by the UI (copy/paste example).  
- Walk through how to create or edit the four JSON files to run a simple single-neuron job programmatically.  
Which would you prefer?

simulate_simple_neuron.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation about single-neuron simulations. I'll search the product docs for \single".## Short answer
Yes — you can simulate one simple biophysical neuron for 1 second on the platform. The Single neuron simulation flow supports that out of the box (the default Duration is 1000 ms).

Below I’ll give a compact step‑by‑step guide you can follow in the web UI, what each step does, and a few practical tips.

---

## Step‑by‑step: run 1 neuron for 1 second

1. Open Workflows → select the **Simulate** category.  
   - This reveals the simulation Types row.

2. Choose **Single neuron** (or **Single neuron (beta)** for the newer flow).  
   - The workspace navigates to a model picker for that simulation type.

3. Pick a model (ME‑model / ME-model / e-model) from the picker:
   - Use the Public or Project tab to find a ready‑to‑simulate neuron model.
   - Click a row to preview morphology / trace; click **Use model** when ready.

4. Configure the simulation:
   - In the **Configuration** tab, open **Setup → Initialization**.
     - Set **Duration** = **1000 ms** (default is already 1000 ms).
     - Optionally set timestep, initial voltage (v_init), random seed, temperature, Ca2+ etc.
   - (Optional) In **Stimuli**, add a current injection (e.g., Constant Somatic Current Clamp).
     - For relative stimuli use % of threshold; for absolute use nA.
     - Set stimulus Duration and timestamp(s).
   - (Optional) In **Recordings**, add a recording (e.g., Soma Voltage Recording) and set sampling timestep (e.g., 0.1 ms).
   - Leave other sections empty if you only want a simple single‑neuron injection and voltage recording.

5. Make sure required fields are filled:
   - **Info** (Campaign Name and Description) are required — fill these so the section turns green.
   - The Generate button becomes enabled when required sections show green checks.

6. Click **Generate simulation(s)**:
   - The platform validates your configuration and creates a simulation campaign.
   - One simulation is produced for the default settings (or one per combination if you used parameter scans).

7. On the **Simulations** tab:
   - Inspect the auto‑generated input files (circuit_config.json / simulation_config.json etc.) if you want.
   - Click **Launch simulations (1)** to submit the simulation for compute.

8. Monitor and download results:
   - After the run completes you can view or download outputs (voltage traces, spike times, other records).

---

## Notes & practical tips

- Default Duration is 1000 ms (1 s). If you use the classic flow the same field is present in Initialization; in the beta flow the same.
- Time step and recording sampling interval are editable (e.g., 0.1 ms sampling is common).
- Stimulus amplitudes: the beta flow offers *Relative* clamps (percentage of each cell's threshold) — convenient for heterogeneous models.
- Parameter scans: numeric fields have a plus (+) to turn them into scan ranges; each extra value multiplies simulations — don’t add scans if you want a single run.
- Generating a campaign does not consume compute credits. Launching (submitting) the simulation consumes credits.
- Use a model whose preview thumbnails render correctly — models with broken thumbnails sometimes fail on generation.
- The interface shows campaign status (Created, Running, Done). “Done” after generation can mean the campaign entity was created — actual compute runs after you click Launch.

---

## Want me to do this for you?
I can walk you through the UI interactively, or prepare a ready campaign configuration (JSON) you can paste into the simulator. Which would you prefer:
- Step‑through in the web UI (I’ll guide you)?
- I prepare the minimal configuration (ME‑model + one constant somatic clamp + recording) you can generate and launch?

## Quick overview — moving an ME-model to simulation
- Open your project and go to **Workflows** → click the **Simulate** category.  
- In the Type row choose the appropriate simulation type for ME-models:
  - For single-cell ME-models use **Single neuron** or **Single neuron (beta)** (the beta flow is the newer section-based UI).
- The workspace opens a model picker scoped to that simulation type. In the picker:
  - Switch between **Public / Project** tabs to find the ME-model.
  - Use search/filters (brain region, m-/e-type, validation/tag fields) to locate the ME-model.
  - Click a row to open the preview (morphology/trace thumbnails, metadata).  
  - Click **Use model** to accept the ME-model and enter the simulation configuration view.
    - (If you want to inspect the model entity first, click **View details**.)

(Reference: \The Workflows tab", \Designing a Simulation", \Configuring a beta simulation")

## Configuration steps (what to fill before generating)
The configuration workspace is a three-column form (left: sections, middle: forms, right: live preview). Required sections vary by type, but common required fields:

1. Setup → **Info**
   - Enter **Campaign name** and **Campaign description** (both required).

2. Setup → **Initialization**
   - Contains global sim parameters (Duration/tstop, dt, v_init, random_seed, extracellular calcium, ME model read-only). Defaults are pre-filled.

3. Stimuli & Recordings
   - Add stimuli (many types available, including relative clamps that scale to each cell's threshold).
   - Add recordings (e.g., Soma Voltage Recording full-duration or time-window).
   - For circuit/beta flows you can target named **Neuron sets**; single-cell has the default All Biophysical Neurons.

4. Manipulations (optional)
   - Ion-channel variable edits, synaptic manipulations, etc.

5. Events / Timestamps (optional but useful)
   - Define named timestamps (Single or Regular) and reference them from stimuli/recordings.

6. Parameter scans
   - Numeric fields have a plus-circle to add scan values; the generated campaign becomes the Cartesian product of scanned values (each extra value multiplies simulations).

Keep filling sections until each required section shows a green check in the left rail. The Generate button enables once required fields are valid.

(Reference: \Designing a Simulation", \Configuring a beta simulation")

## Generate → inspect inputs → launch compute
- Click **Generate simulation(s)** (bottom of left column).
  - The platform validates the configuration and creates a Simulation Campaign.
- Workspace switches to the **Simulations** tab where each generated simulation appears (Simulation 0 … N).
  - You can open and view the four main JSON input artifacts (circuit_config.json, node_sets.json, obi_one_coordinate.json, simulation_config.json) in the JSON viewer.
- Click **Launch simulations (N)** to submit for compute. *This is the action that consumes credits*.
- Use the **Activities** view (project home → Activities or Workflows listing) to inspect campaign/campaign status and results once runs complete.

Tips from the docs:
- Pick models whose preview thumbnails render correctly (broken thumbnails often indicate incomplete entities and can cause backend failures).
- Defaults are usually sufficient for a smoke test—filling Info can often produce a single-simulation campaign.
- Save/copy the campaign UUID (Copy simulation campaign ID) if you need to reference the campaign via API/CLI or re-open it later.

(Reference: \Generating a small microcircuit (beta) campaign", \Designing a Simulation")

## Where to find and run simulation notebooks (ME-model notebooks)
- The project top navigation includes a **Notebooks** tab. Open **Notebooks** to browse runnable notebooks in your project.
- The **Project Home** Get started cards include a **Notebooks** highlight with quick links (View Notebooks examples) that open Quick access → Notebooks.
- Quick access shows curated notebook examples; use it to find example notebooks for running ME-model simulations or post-processing simulation outputs.
- Tutorials and “How to” videos (Project Home → Tutorials or Help → Tutorials) include walkthroughs demonstrating simulation design and running notebooks; use the Tutorials carousel or the See all / Filter by: Simulate to locate relevant videos.
- The AI Assistant (right-side drawer) can also suggest context-aware prompts and point you to notebooks or examples relevant to the ME-model or brain region you’re viewing.

Notes:
- Notebooks are project-scoped — open the Notebooks tab in the project that contains your ME-model/campaign to see available notebooks.
- Many projects ship runnable example notebooks that show how to load the SONATA outputs (the four JSON files) and run analyses/visualizations; copy the campaign’s simulation_config.json and other artifacts into the notebook workspace or point notebooks at the persisted campaign results.

(Reference: \The project home", \The Workflows tab")

## Practical checklist (short)
- Workflows → Simulate → choose Single neuron (or Single neuron (beta)).
- Picker → select your ME-model → **Use model**.
- Fill Info, Initialization, Stimuli, Recordings (and optional manipulations/timestamps).
- Generate simulation(s) → review the JSON input files in Simulations tab.
- Launch simulations to run (consumes credits).
- Notebooks tab / Quick access / Tutorials → open simulation notebooks to load results and run analyses.

## Credit & troubleshooting notes
- Launching simulations uses project compute credits. The project home shows a credits counter.
- If Generate fails or the Simulations tab doesn’t activate:
  - Try a different model (prefer one with working thumbnails), inspect browser console for backend errors, or check Activities for persisted campaigns.
  - The docs warn some broken models cause backend 500s; choose models with rendered previews to reduce failures.

---

synaptic_input_meaning.user: What does the "synaptic input" on the current page mean? Is it a signal input from a specified position?
synaptic_input_meaning.expected_output: In the current Simulation -> Synaptime you can stimulate existing sets of synapses on the synaptome. You can build such a synaptome with specific locations, through Build > Synaptome.
synaptic_input_meaning.ai_response:
I'll search the platform documentation for the meaning of \synaptic input.\ I'll look up product documentation.## Short answer
On this platform, **“synaptic input”** refers to inputs delivered by synapses in the model (the connection/edge-level inputs defined in the circuit), not a single external signal injected at an arbitrary spatial coordinate. It means activity that arrives via the model’s synaptic connections — either from other neurons in the circuit or from external/virtual input populations — and can be modified by the synaptic-manipulations controls.

## Details and how it maps to the docs
- Circuit models on the platform are represented as SONATA-style circuits: the circuit config declares populations (networks.nodes) and connection files (networks.edges). The docs show examples such as internal hippocampus_neurons__hippocampus_neurons__chemical_synapse and external_hippocampus_neurons__hippocampus_neurons__chemical_synapse for those sources of synaptic input.
- The configuration UI separates:
  - Stimuli (Stimuli & Recordings → Stimuli): these are current injections (somatic clamps and other waveform types) that you explicitly add in the Stimuli section. These are not labeled “synaptic input” — they are direct current or conductance injections you place via the Stimuli form.
  - Synaptic manipulations (Manipulations → Synaptic manipulations, present for circuit-scale/beta flows): these let you change synapse-level properties during the simulation (for example, scaling synaptic conductances, blocking pathways, toggling short-term plasticity). The docs explicitly describe Synaptic manipulations as modifying synapse-level properties.
- In other words:
  - “Synaptic input” = activity that enters a neuron via its synapses (determined by the circuit’s connectivity / edge files and any external virtual populations).
  - It is not the same as the Stimulus controls (which are direct current/clamp inputs you add in the Stimuli section).
  - It is not described in the docs as a “signal from a specified spatial position.” Instead, it is routed by the model’s connectivity (which can include external populations or specific presynaptic cells); you can target subsets of the circuit using neuron sets where that UI is available.

## Practical implications / what you can do in the UI
- If you want to *inject* a waveform at a particular neuron or subpopulation, use Stimuli and set the Neuron set (available on paired/circuit flows) to choose the target cells.
- If you want to *modify* or *block* synaptic inputs, use Synaptic manipulations to scale conductances or disable pathways defined by the model’s edges (this affects synapses defined in the circuit).
- If you intended to deliver a spatially localized input (e.g., a synaptic event at a specific morphological compartment or x,y,z position), the docs do not describe a direct “place-by-position” stimulus in the beta UI — inputs are targeted by neuron sets or arise from the model’s presynaptic populations/edges. If you need compartment- or location-specific synaptic activation, tell me which model/page you’re on and I can show whether the model supports per-compartment targeting or how to configure an equivalent via neuron sets or edge/virtual populations.

Would you like me to:
- Point to the exact controls on the page you’re looking at (tell me which page/model), or
- Walk through how to add a synaptic manipulation or a neuron-set–targeted stimulus for your model?

run_simulation_file.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for running simulations (documentationProduct).## Short answer — two ways

- If your file is a platform model / ME- or E-model (registered in the project): use Workflows → Simulate → pick the appropriate simulation type (Single neuron or Single neuron (beta)), select the model in the model picker, configure the campaign, Generate simulation(s), then click Launch simulations to run it.
- If your file is a local Python script you want to execute as arbitrary code: run it inside a project Notebook (open Notebooks → New Notebook, upload the file, run it there). Notebooks run arbitrary Python but are separate from the platform’s simulator workflows.

Below are step-by-step instructions for both routes and tips for debugging and retrieving results.

---

## A. Run it as a simulator workflow (recommended if file is an ME-/E-model or registered model entity)

Use this when the file is a model entity on the platform (ME-model, E-model, or an entity that can be used by the simulator).

1. Open the project that should run the simulation.
2. Go to Workflows → click the Simulate category.
3. Choose the correct simulation Type:
   - For single-neuron files choose “Single neuron” or “Single neuron (beta)” (beta has the modern section-based form).
   - For circuit-scale models choose the appropriate circuit type.
4. In the model picker:
   - Use Public / Project tabs to find the ME/E-model or simulator-ready model that corresponds to your file.
   - Click the row → preview panel → confirm the model.
   - Click Use model to open the configuration workspace.
5. Configure the campaign:
   - Fill Info (Campaign name + Campaign description) — required.
   - Initialization — set duration, dt, initial voltage, extracellular Ca, random seed, etc.
   - Stimuli — add current clamps or other stimuli (Relative clamps are often recommended).
   - Recordings — add somatic voltage recordings (dt).
   - (Beta) Add neuron sets, manipulations, timestamps as needed.
   - Use parameter-scans (plus icon) if you want multiple simulations.
   - Watch the section icons on the left; all required sections must show green checks.
6. Click Generate simulation(s):
   - This creates the campaign and the per-simulation JSON input files (circuit_config.json, node_sets.json, obi_one_coordinate.json, simulation_config.json).
   - The workspace switches to the Simulations tab where you can inspect the generated JSON.
7. Launch the simulation(s):
   - In the Simulations tab click the green Launch simulations (N) button; this is the action that actually submits compute and consumes credits.
8. Monitor & retrieve results:
   - After runs complete, the results are available from the campaign’s page (Activities / campaign detail). You can download outputs from there.
   - Copy the campaign UUID if you need to access results via the API or entity SDK.

Notes / tips from the docs:
- Defaults are usually OK for a smoke test — filling Info + Generate will produce a one-simulation campaign.
- Broken thumbnails in the model picker are a leading indicator that the model might fail when generating campaigns; pick a model that renders a working preview.
- Generate persists the campaign but does not run it — Launch simulations is required to run compute.

Reference: “Designing a Simulation”, “Configuring a beta simulation”, “Generating a small microcircuit (beta) campaign” (documentationProduct).

---

## B. Run a local Python script (passive_neuron_1dend.py) inside a Notebook

Use this when you want to run arbitrary Python code (e.g., custom single-cell script not registered as a platform model).

1. Open the project.
2. Go to Notebooks → New Notebook (choose Python kernel available).
3. Upload passive_neuron_1dend.py to the notebook (File → Upload or drag & drop).
4. In a notebook cell either:
   - %run passive_neuron_1dend.py
   - or import functions from the script and run them programmatically
5. Execute cells to run the simulation code and inspect results/plots inline.
6. Save the notebook or export outputs.

Notes:
- Running in a Notebook executes arbitrary Python but does not automatically create a simulator campaign entity.
- Use Notebooks if you need custom analysis, visualization, or want to run code that is not already integrated with the platform’s SONATA/obi-one simulator flow.

---

## Which approach should you pick?

- If passive_neuron_1dend.py is already wrapped as a platform model (ME/E-model) or you want to use the platform’s simulator, do A (Workflows → Simulate). This gives reproducible SONATA input artifacts, campaign entities, and easier downstream tracking.
- If the script is a stand-alone Python experiment or you need to iterate quickly with custom code, do B (Notebooks).

If you’d like, tell me:
- Is passive_neuron_1dend.py already registered as a model entity on the platform (ME-model / E-model)? — or —
- Is it a local script you want to upload and run inside a Notebook?

- Not directly. The platform currently supports cellular- and small-circuit-level simulations (single neurons, synaptome, paired neurons, small microcircuits and their beta flows).  
- **Brain-region / brain-system / whole-brain** build-and-simulate types are *present in the UI roadmap but disabled in the current release* (they are not yet available for launch). The documentation lists those types as coming later.  
- For surgical planning of a Chiari malformation (an anatomical/biomechanical problem), you will almost certainly need patient-specific 3D anatomy and biomechanical / fluid-dynamics modelling (CSF flow, tissue deformation), which the platform does not yet expose as a turnkey whole-brain / biomechanics workflow.

Below I explain what you *can* do on the platform today, what is currently unavailable, and practical hybrid workflows you can use for planning now.

---

## What the platform currently supports (relevant doc references)
- Simulation workflows described in “The Workflows tab” and “Designing a Simulation”: enabled simulation types today are:
  - **Single neuron**, **Synaptome**
  - Beta flows: **Single neuron (beta)**, **Synaptome (beta)**, **Paired neurons (beta)**, **Small microcircuit (beta)**

---

## Why Chiari malformation simulation is different from the platform’s current remit
- Chiari malformation is primarily an anatomical/biomechanical / fluid-dynamics problem (cerebellar tonsils, posterior fossa anatomy, CSF flow dynamics, possible syringomyelia). Surgical planning typically requires:
  - Patient-specific MRI/CT-derived 3D meshes
  - Tissue mechanics (finite-element models) and/or CFD for CSF
  - Interaction with bone/skull/base-of-skull geometry
- The platform’s available simulation types today are electrophysiology / circuit simulation oriented and do not provide built-in whole-brain anatomy, finite-element deformation, or clinical CFD modules needed for surgical planning.

---

## Practical approaches you can take now

1. Short-term: study neural-function consequences using platform-supported scales
   - Use **Small microcircuit (beta)** or **Paired neurons (beta)** to model how local cerebellar or brainstem circuits might change electrophysiologically under specific perturbations (e.g., altered input patterns, altered extracellular conditions).

     2. Pick an available model (preview it, then Use model).

     4. Click **Generate simulation(s)** — the workflow validates and creates a campaign.
     5. On the Simulations tab, download the produced JSON files (circuit_config.json / simulation_config.json, etc.) or launch compute on the platform.
   - Use this to test hypotheses about neural activity changes **but not** to plan surgical decompression or predict CSF/tissue mechanics.

2. Hybrid / external workflow for surgical planning (recommended)
   - Create patient-specific 3D anatomical models (segmented MRI/CT) and run biomechanical / CFD simulations in dedicated clinical or research tools (e.g., finite-element solvers, CFD packages, or clinical planning software).

I'll check the platform documentation for simulation workflows. I'll pull the product docs for \simulation".## How to run a multi‑neuron (circuit / small microcircuit) simulation on the platform

Below is a concise, step‑by‑step guide based on the platform documentation for the Simulate workflow (beta circuit flows such as **Paired neurons (beta)** and **Small microcircuit (beta)**).

---

## 1) Start the Simulate workflow
- Open your project and go to the **Workflows** tab.
- Click the **Simulate** category card to reveal the Type row.
- Click the circuit‑scale Type you want (for multi‑neuron: **Paired neurons (beta)** or **Small microcircuit (beta)**).  
  This opens the model picker for that simulation type and starts a draft session (no compute/credits used yet).

---

## 2) Pick a multi‑neuron model
- In the model picker:
  - Use the **Public / Project** tabs to choose platform models vs your project models.
  - Search or filter (brain region, name, etc.) to find a suitable circuit.
  - Click a row to open the preview panel — confirm the morphology, connectivity, and metadata (number of neurons / synapses / connections).
- Click **Use model** to accept the model and open the simulation configuration view.

Tip: Pick a model whose preview thumbnail renders properly — broken thumbnails often indicate incomplete models that can fail generation.

---

## 3) Configure the campaign (section‑based beta form)
The configuration workspace is a three‑column layout:
- Left: section navigator (status icons and Generate button)
- Middle: active section form
- Right: live preview of the circuit

Sections (typical for circuit beta types):
- **Setup**
  - **Info** (required) — Campaign Name and Campaign Description (both required)
  - **Initialization** — global simulation settings (duration, dt, v_init, extracellular calcium, random seed). Defaults are prefilled.
- **Stimuli & Recordings**
  - **Stimuli** — add current injections (many types: relative to threshold, absolute, OU, sinusoidal, etc.). For circuits you can target neuron sets (e.g., presynaptic cell only).
  - **Recordings** — define what to record (soma voltage, compartments, time window, sampling timestep).
- **Circuit Components**
  - **Neuron sets** — define named subsets of neurons to target with stimuli/recordings/manipulations.
- **Manipulations**
  - For circuits: **Synaptic manipulations** — scale conductances, block pathways, toggle STP, etc.
- **Events**
  - **Timestamps** — named time markers that stimuli/recordings can reference.

Important:
- Sections show status icons: green check = valid, yellow warning = required fields missing (blocks Generate).
- Fill **Info** and any other required fields so all required sections show green.

---

## 4) Parameter scans (optional)
- Many numeric fields have a small plus icon to add scan values. Each scanned value produces additional simulations — the campaign becomes the Cartesian product of scanned values.
- Scan sparingly: each extra value multiplies the number of simulations.

---

## 5) Generate the campaign (create simulation(s))
- When every required section is green, the **Generate simulation(s)** button becomes enabled.
- Click **Generate simulation(s)**:
  - The platform validates the configuration.
  - On success, a **campaign** is created and one or more **Simulation** rows appear (one per combination of scanned parameters).
  - The UI switches to the **Simulations** tab for that campaign.

What is produced (visible in the Simulations tab):
- Input files you can inspect/copy:
  - `circuit_config.json` (SONATA circuit config)
  - `node_sets.json`
  - `obi_one_coordinate.json` (scanned-parameter axes)
  - `simulation_config.json` (SONATA simulation config; Initialization and stimuli/recordings go here)
- A **Copy simulation campaign ID** button (useful for API/CLI or reopening the campaign).

Note: The campaign entity being created (status Done) ≠ the simulation having run. The campaign being persisted is separate from actually launching compute.

---

## 6) Launch compute (run simulations)
- In the Simulations tab:
  - Select the simulations you want (Select all or individual rows).
  - Click **Launch simulations (N)** in the footer to submit selected simulations for execution.
  - Launching consumes compute credits and creates runtime jobs you can monitor (status, logs, outputs).

---

## 7) Inspect results and downloads
- After runs complete, you can view and download outputs from the campaign/simulation results (traces, files, etc.) via the campaign detail or Activities listing.
- Use the campaign UUID (Copy simulation campaign ID) to access the entity via API/CLI if needed.

---

## Quick checklist (practical)
- [ ] Pick a model with a working preview (thumbnail + connectivity graph).
- [ ] Fill Campaign Name and Description (Info section).
- [ ] Verify Initialization values (duration, dt, v_init, seed).
- [ ] Add any Stimuli and Recordings you need; define Neuron sets if targeting subsets.
- [ ] Add Synaptic manipulations if you want intervention experiments.
- [ ] Add scans only when necessary (be mindful of combinatorial explosion).
- [ ] Click Generate → inspect generated JSONs.
- [ ] Select simulation(s) → Click Launch (consumes credits).
- [ ] Monitor run status and download outputs from the campaign / Activities.

---

## Tips & caveats from the docs
- Defaults are usually enough for a smoke test — filling Info alone often produces a single valid simulation.
- If Generate silently fails or Simulations tab does not become active, try a different model (some public models can cause backend 500s).
- Use **Relative** stimuli to scale injection amplitudes to each cell's threshold across heterogeneous populations.
- Save the campaign UUID — it’s the stable handle for API/CLI access.
- The AI Assistant and tutorials in the project can provide context‑aware help for common tasks.

---

If you’d like, I can:
- Walk through a concrete example (e.g., create a small microcircuit campaign with specific stimuli), or
- Open the Workflows documentation for a particular simulation Type and extract the exact default Initialization values or available stimulus types. Which would you prefer?

sst_ipsp_amplitude.user: What is the amplitude of the inhibitory postsynaptic potentials of SST cells over pyramidal cells or PV cells in the cortex?
sst_ipsp_amplitude.expected_output:
This data is not directly available on the platform. However, here is what the literature says:

{{literature_search_results}}

arguments.query: amplitude inhibitory postsynaptic potential SST somatostatin cells pyramidal PV cortex

sst_ipsp_amplitude.ai_response:
## Short answer — typical amplitudes
- Unitary inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (uIPSPs) produced by somatostatin-expressing (SST) interneurons onto cortical pyramidal cells at the soma (measured in acute slices) are typically **small to moderate**, on the order of **~0.2 to ~1.5 mV** at the somatic recording site (most often < 1 mV when measured at the soma).  
- SST → PV (parvalbumin) cell unitary IPSPs are generally **weak to moderate and often smaller** than PV→pyramidal perisomatic IPSPs; typical somatic uIPSP amplitudes are often **≤ 1 mV**, frequently in the **~0.1–1.0 mV** range.  
- By contrast, PV interneuron → pyramidal cell unitary IPSPs (perisomatic targeting) are commonly **larger**, often **~1–3 mV** or higher at the soma in similar preparations.

These are approximate ranges — exact amplitudes depend strongly on experimental conditions (species, age, cortical area, slice vs in vivo, temperature), recording location (somatic vs dendritic), and whether amplitude is reported raw or corrected for driving force and series resistance.

## Key points and interpretation
- Target location matters: SST interneurons mainly target distal dendrites of pyramidal cells. Because of dendritic filtering, an otherwise physiologically significant dendritic IPSC can appear quite small when recorded at the soma. Thus:
  - Somatic uIPSP amplitude underestimates the local dendritic inhibitory effect of SST synapses.
  - SST inhibition is particularly effective at controlling dendritic excitability, synaptic integration and calcium signals, even when somatic uIPSPs are small.
- Cell-type specificity: SST → PV connections exist but are often sparser and weaker than PV → pyramidal connections. Functional roles differ: SST cells control dendritic integration and modulatory gating; PV cells provide strong, fast perisomatic inhibition that potently controls spike timing.
- Synaptic conductance vs voltage deflection: Reports sometimes give synaptic conductances or IPSC amplitudes (pA) rather than somatic voltage deflections (mV). Converting between these requires knowledge of input resistance and driving force.

## Practical considerations (for experimental planning or interpretation)
- If you measure uIPSPs at the soma, expect SST→pyramidal values often <1 mV; consider dendritic recordings, voltage-clamp IPSCs, or optical sensors to assess local dendritic inhibition more directly.
- Temperature and extracellular [Ca2+] strongly modulate synaptic release probability and hence uIPSP amplitudes; recordings at physiological temperature and physiologic ionic conditions usually give larger and more reliable amplitudes than room-temperature slice recordings.
- Use averaged paired recordings (many trials) to quantify mean amplitude and failure rate; report median and interquartile ranges because distributions are often skewed.

If you want, I can:
- Look up published paired-recording measurements for a specific cortex (e.g., mouse somatosensory cortex, layer 2/3) and give exact reported amplitudes from the literature.
- Or search the Open Brain Platform / EntityCore databases for circuit datasets that contain measured SST→pyramidal or SST→PV synaptic amplitudes. Which would you prefer?

## 1) Clarify goals and scale (quick questions)

## 2) Suggested model manipulations to represent aging and TBI

Below are commonly used, platform-feasible manipulations you can include. I’ll incorporate whichever you choose when drafting the simulation config.

- Aging hippocampus (examples):

  - Synapse loss: reduce number or strength of excitatory synapses (e.g., scaling synaptic weights or connection probabilities).
  - Dendritic atrophy: switch m-types to ones with reduced dendritic extent or scale morphology.
  - Ion channel expression changes: reduce H‑channel or Ca2+ channel conductances, or change membrane time constants to reflect slowed dynamics.
  - Increased excitability heterogeneity / noise.
  - Changes in inhibitory interneuron function (reduced PV interneuron efficacy).
- TBI in adult hippocampus (examples):
  - Focal lesion: remove neurons inside a volume (lesion core) and reduce connectivity crossing that volume.
  - Diffuse axonal damage: reduce long-range axonal efficacy and increase conduction delays.
  - Local inflammation: increase baseline excitability / reduce inhibition.
  - Synaptic reorganization: increase aberrant recurrent excitatory connectivity (could cause hyperexcitability/seizure-like dynamics).

## 3) Recommended experimental design (example)

brain_structures_movement.user: What are the structures of the brain mainly involved in movement?
brain_structures_movement.ai_response:
## Summary — main brain structures involved in movement

Below is a concise overview of the principal structures and circuits that generate, plan, refine and transmit motor commands.

### Cortical motor areas
- **Primary motor cortex (M1; Brodmann area 4)**  
  - Role: Executes voluntary movements; sends descending corticospinal and corticobulbar projections to brainstem and spinal motor neurons.  
  - Functional note: Somatotopic organization (motor homunculus).

- **Premotor cortex**  
  - Role: Plans and selects movements in response to external cues; coordinates proximal and trunk muscles.

- **Supplementary motor area (SMA)**  
  - Role: Plans internally generated sequences, coordinates bilateral and complex movements.

### Basal ganglia (BG) — action selection and movement vigor
- **Components:** **striatum** (caudate + putamen), **globus pallidus** (internal GPi, external GPe), **subthalamic nucleus (STN)**, **substantia nigra** (pars compacta SNc, pars reticulata SNr).  
- **Role:** Initiation and inhibition of movements, selection among competing motor programs, scaling movement amplitude and velocity.  
- **Neurochemistry:** Dopamine from SNc critically modulates BG function.  
- **Clinical relevance:** Dopaminergic loss → **Parkinson’s disease** (bradykinesia, rigidity); STN/GPi dysfunction → dyskinesias.

### Cerebellum — coordination and motor learning
- **Role:** Coordinates timing, accuracy and smoothness of movements; error correction, motor adaptation, procedural learning.  
- **Pathways:** Receives sensory/proprioceptive input and cortical “efference copy”; sends corrective signals via deep cerebellar nuclei → thalamus → cortex.  
- **Clinical relevance:** Lesions → ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor.

### Thalamus — relay and integration
- **Role:** Relays processed motor signals from basal ganglia and cerebellum back to motor and premotor cortices (thalamocortical loops), enabling cortical execution and modulation.

### Brainstem motor centers
- **Components/roles:**  
  - **Red nucleus:** rubrospinal contributions (more prominent in some species), coordination of limb movement.  
  - **Vestibular nuclei & reticular formation:** control posture, balance, and automatic/tonic components of movement; mediate vestibulospinal and reticulospinal tracts.  
  - **Cranial nerve motor nuclei:** control muscles of face, eyes, jaw, pharynx and larynx.

### Spinal cord — final common pathway
- **Role:** Contains lower (alpha and gamma) motor neurons that directly innervate skeletal muscle; integrates reflexes and local pattern generators (e.g., central pattern generators for locomotion).

### Sensory & proprioceptive systems (supporting role)
- **Proprioceptive pathways** (muscle spindles, Golgi tendon organs → dorsal columns/spinocerebellar tracts) provide feedback crucial for coordinated movement and corrective adjustments.

### Major functional circuits (high level)
- **Cortico–spinal and cortico–bulbar pathways:** direct cortical drive to spinal and brainstem motor neurons.  
- **Basal ganglia — thalamo — cortical loop:** gating and selecting motor programs; modulating movement initiation and vigor.  
- **Cerebello — thalamo — cortical loop:** error correction, timing and motor learning.  
- **Brainstem — spinal tracts (vestibulo-/reticulo-/rubrospinal):** posture, tone and automatic movements.

## Quick reference table

| Structure / System | Main role |
|---|---|
| **Primary motor cortex (M1)** | Execution of voluntary movement; corticospinal output |
| **Premotor cortex / SMA** | Motor planning, sequencing, bilateral coordination |
| **Basal ganglia** | Action selection, initiation/inhibition, scaling movement |
| **Cerebellum** | Coordination, timing, motor learning & error correction |
| **Thalamus** | Relay/integration for BG & cerebellar outputs to cortex |
| **Brainstem nuclei** | Posture, balance, automatic motor control, cranial motor output |
| **Spinal cord** | Lower motor neurons, reflexes, pattern generators |
| **Proprioceptive pathways** | Feedback for adjustment and coordination |

## Clinical examples (to connect structure → symptom)
- Parkinson’s disease: impaired basal ganglia (dopamine loss) → bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor.  
- Cerebellar lesion: ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor.  
- Corticospinal (M1) lesion: weakness or paralysis of contralateral voluntary movements; spasticity over time.  
- Spinal cord injury: loss of voluntary movement and reflex changes below lesion.

If you want, I can:
- Show a diagram of these circuits, or
- Describe in more detail any single structure (e.g., basal ganglia pathways, cerebellar anatomy, corticospinal tract), or
- Provide references and key review papers. Which would you like next?

entitysdk_library.ai_response:
I'll fetch the official documentation for the entitysdk package.## Summary — What is **entitysdk**

**entitysdk** is the official Python SDK for interacting with the Open Brain Platform's EntityCore service. It provides a type-safe, Pydantic-based client and models to search, create, update, and manage neuroscience-related entities (e.g., cell morphologies, circuits, simulations, ion channel models) and their associated assets via the platform's REST API.

---

## Key features

- **Client class** for authenticated access and project-scoped operations (handles API URL, project context, token management).
- **Pydantic models** that represent EntityCore entity types (e.g., CellMorphology, SimulationCampaign), enabling type-safe requests and responses.
- **CRUD operations** for entities (search, create, update, delete).
- **File management** utilities:
  - Single-file uploads
  - Directory uploads
  - Multipart uploads for large files
  - Streaming downloads
- **Support for multiple environments** (e.g., staging, production) and project/virtual-lab scoping through a ProjectContext object.
- **Helpers** for working with complex nested data and for iterating search results.

---

## Typical usage (examples from the SDK docs)

### Initialize client and project context
```python
from entitysdk import Client, ProjectContext
import os

entitycore_api_url = \http://127.0.0.1:8000"
project_context = ProjectContext(
    virtual_lab_id=\a98b7abc-fc46-4700-9e3d-37137812c730",
    project_id=\0dbced5f-cc3d-488a-8c7f-cfb8ea039dc6",
)
token = os.getenv(\ACCESS_TOKEN", \XXX")
client = Client(api_url=entitycore_api_url, project_context=project_context, token_manager=token)
```

### Search entities (iterator pattern)
```python
from uuid import UUID
from entitysdk import models

iterator = client.search_entity(
    entity_type=models.CellMorphology,
    query={\mtype__pref_label": \L5_TPC:A"},
    limit=1,
)
morphology = next(iterator)
```

### Upload a file to an entity
```python
client.upload_file(
    entity_id=morphology.id,
    entity_type=models.CellMorphology,
    file_path=\path/to/file.swc",
    file_content_type=\application/swc",
)
```

### Additional examples in notebooks
The SDK repository includes example notebooks (e.g., analysis notebooks, circuit examples) that show how to:
- initialize and configure runtime info (Docker/Python/OS runtime metadata) for analysis notebooks,
- manage templates and dependencies,
- use the Client to orchestrate entity operations in analysis workflows.

---

## Internal design notes (from docs)

- The SDK centers around a `Client` object that encapsulates authentication, the project context, and all entity operations.
- Models are declared with Pydantic for validation and type safety.
- The SDK exposes helpers to ease working with nested structures and common entity management tasks used in Open Brain Platform notebooks and workflows.

---

## Where to find more

The SDK documentation and examples (README, example notebooks) are available in the repository and include runnable notebook examples demonstrating initialization, search, and file upload use-cases.

If you want, I can:
- Show exact code snippets for a specific entity type (e.g., CellMorphology, Circuit).
- Open the example notebook that demonstrates analysis notebook initialization.
- Explain best practices for authentication and token handling in notebooks. Which would you like next?

platform_purpose_applications.ai_response:
I'll check the platform documentation for an official description and intended audience.## Summary — What this platform is for

The **Open Brain Platform (OBP)** is a cloud-based virtual lab for building, analysing and running biophysically‑detailed brain models and associated data workflows. Its core purpose is to let researchers and teams browse validated experimental and model data (morphologies, ion‑channel recordings, circuits, etc.), assemble reproducible simulation campaigns (single neurons, paired neurons, synaptomes, small microcircuits and larger circuits), run those simulations at scale, and inspect / share results (notebooks, reports).

## Who it’s designed for

- **Neuroscientists and computational neuroscientists** who build and validate biophysical neuron and circuit models.  
- **Modellers and simulation engineers** who need to assemble SONATA-style simulation configs, launch campaigns and manage compute.  
- **Labs and research groups (virtual lab / project model)** — authenticated users work inside projects that own data, simulations, notebooks and credits.  
- **Educators and trainees** who need guided tutorials, glossary and example workflows to learn modelling workflows.  
(These user roles are implied across the product documentation, workflow guides and Help pages.)

## Key capabilities (platform purpose expressed as features)

- Centralized data catalogue: browse, search and upload experimental and model artifacts (morphologies, ion channel recordings, ME‑models, circuits, synaptome data).  
- Workflow-driven simulation: section‑based configuration forms to design simulation campaigns (Single neuron, Paired neurons, Synaptome, Small microcircuit, etc.), generate SONATA input files and launch compute.  
- Reproducible campaigns: generated artifacts include circuit_config.json, node_sets.json, simulation_config.json and campaign metadata that are persisted and can be re‑launched or inspected.  
- Analysis & reproducibility: Jupyter notebooks integration (JupyterHub), reports and saved campaign artifacts for downstream analysis.  
- AI Assistant + tools: integrated assistant exposing platform tools (web search, literature search, entity lookups, compute helpers, state management) and a Help center with tutorials, glossary and guides.  
- Metrics & analysis helpers (documented tool surface): compute morphology and electrophysiology metrics, compute circuit metrics and connectivity metrics, analyze circuit populations (tool catalogue lists these compute/analysis capabilities).

## Real‑world applications

- Reproducible single‑cell and circuit modelling research: build validated single‑cell biophysical models, test parameter variations, run controlled simulation campaigns and publish or share the resulting data and configs.  
- Microcircuit and synaptome studies: assemble small microcircuits or synaptome models to study connectivity motifs, synaptic counts, excitation/inhibition balance and network dynamics.  
- Comparative model validation: compare morphology and electrophysiology metrics across experimental recordings and candidate model variants (validation workflows + metric tools).  
- Collaborative lab workflows: projects hold shared datasets, campaigns and notebooks so multi‑person teams can reproduce and extend simulations.  
- Teaching and training: guided tutorials, glossary and prebuilt examples (e.g., “How to build a Single Cell model”, “How to simulate a small microcircuit”) support hands‑on learning.  
- Method development and pipeline automation: generate SONATA-compatible artifacts for downstream workflows, iterate model building and parameter scans, and export campaign JSONs for offline use or continuous integration.

## Notes and operational points (from the docs)

- Simulation campaigns and generated files are persisted to projects; actual compute is only consumed after explicitly launching simulations (credits are used at launch).  
- The Help page contains tutorials, glossaries, feature catalogue and an AI Chat Tools index to learn the platform workflows and available tools.  
- The platform is organized around virtual labs → projects → Data / Workflows / Notebooks / Reports; projects own data and campaigns.

I'll look for documentation pages about simulations, circuits, and supported species.I'll search the documentation for species and supported organisms on the platform.## Short answer — today’s status
The Open Brain Platform currently supports building and simulating models for **mouse, rat, and human** (see the platform docs: **Species Selection**). It does **not** yet provide native, catalogued support for Drosophila / fly as a species. Because of that, you cannot directly build a fully platform-native “fly brain” using only the platform’s public model catalogue today.

Below I outline:
- What the platform CAN do today that is relevant,
- Why a direct fly brain build is blocked,
- Practical options to proceed (workarounds, data prep, and next steps),
- A concrete checklist of files and steps to make a fly model importable once support or a project-specific workflow is available.

---

## What the platform already offers that’s useful
- A section-based simulation workflow (Workflows → Simulate) for building and running campaigns (single neuron, synaptome, paired neurons, small microcircuit and beta variants).  
  - See: **Designing a Simulation** and **Configuring a beta simulation**.
- The platform persists campaigns as SONATA-style artifacts and exposes the four canonical input files after generation:
  - **circuit_config.json** (SONATA circuit config)
  - **node_sets.json** (named node-set definitions)
  - **obi_one_coordinate.json** (campaign coordinates / parameter scan axes)
  - **simulation_config.json** (SONATA simulation config)
  - See: **Generating a small microcircuit (beta) campaign**.
- The platform requires models with working previews (morphology thumbnails etc.) to reliably generate campaigns.

These points are useful because they define the import format and runtime expectations you must meet to run a circuit on the platform.

---

## Why you can’t build a fly brain directly right now
- Platform species support is limited to **mouse, rat, human** (docs: *Species Selection*). Fly is not a supported species in the public catalogue, so there are no ready-to-use fly neuron/circuit entities to pick in the model picker.
- As a result, you cannot select fly models from the model picker or run a campaign from a public fly circuit until the platform adds fly species support or you register project-specific fly entities that the platform will accept.

---

## Practical options / recommended paths forward

1) Request platform support for fly (recommended for community-scale work)
   - Use the platform Help / Feedback to request Drosophila support (or open a support ticket). Provide use cases, example connectome datasets you want supported, and typical scales you need (full brain, neuropil circuits, EM-derived synaptomes). The platform team can prioritize species support or accept contributions.
   - Rationale: once fly is supported, the model picker, previews, and simulation backend will accept fly circuits and let you run the standard workflows.

2) Prepare a project-scoped import (project-only models)
   - If you have project write access, you may be able to upload your own model entities (morphologies, circuit files) into your project and use them in the beta simulation flows. The platform requires valid previews and SONATA-style wiring to generate campaigns successfully.
   - Key requirement: uploaded models must match the platform’s expected artifact structure (see checklist below). Models with broken thumbnails or missing SONATA mappings often fail to generate.

3) Work offline until platform support is added
   - Export or transform fly connectome data into SONATA-compatible files locally (nodes, edges, node_types, morphologies). Iterate and validate locally with SONATA-compatible tools/simulators, then import to the platform when ready.

4) Hybrid approach — ask platform team about assisted ingestion
   - Contact support to see if they can help ingest a fly connectome as project-private circuit entities (the platform team sometimes helps import large or new-species datasets for collaborators).

---

## Technical checklist — prepare your fly connectome so it will be importable/usable on the platform

(These items follow the platform’s SONATA-style campaign expectations shown in the docs.)

1. Circuit and population files (SONATA-style)
   - circuit_config.json
     - Declare networks.nodes and networks.edges entries and component directories.
     - Reference biophysical model and morphology directories and id_mapping provenance.
   - node_types.csv / nodes.h5 (node metadata) with fields used by platform filters (e.g., mtype, layer, e-type) — include categorical values as strings.
   - edges.* (edge files) listing pre/post IDs and synapse-level details.

2. Morphologies and model templates
   - Per-neuron morphology files (SWC / NeuroMorpho / other formats accepted by your pipeline).
   - Electrical/biophysical templates (NEURON hoc/mod or other accepted templates).
   - Ensure each morphology has a working thumbnail/preview image — the platform’s UI uses thumbnails as a proxy for “valid model” and models with broken thumbnails often fail to generate.

3. Node sets
   - node_sets.json: named neuron groups (e.g., “All Biophysical Neurons”, specific neuropil compartments or cell classes) so stimuli/recordings and manipulations can target them in the UI.

4. SONATA simulation_config.json
   - Global simulation parameters (tstop, dt, seed, v_init, extracellular calcium if used).
   - Stimuli/recordings placeholders (these can be filled in the web form if desired).

5. Metadata and attribution
   - Brain region annotation (if the platform requires mapping to a brain-region ID; for fly, note that the platform currently won’t have a matching brain-region hierarchy).
   - License and contributor metadata.

6. Validation and preview
   - Create preview thumbnails for morphologies and (where possible) sample traces.
   - Test generation locally or with a minimal SONATA run to ensure all file references and template paths are consistent.

7. Parameter scans (optional)
   - If you want to generate montages of simulations, ensure numeric fields that you want to scan are properly represented so the platform’s scan UI can create the Cartesian product.

---

## How to proceed right now — step-by-step recommended workflow
1. Decide whether you want:
   - (A) Platform-native fly support (ask platform team), or
   - (B) Project-private import of your fly models (prepare the artifacts below).
2. Prepare your circuit in SONATA format (see checklist). Produce working morphology thumbnails and ensure paths and templates are consistent.
3. Contact platform support / file a Help → Feedback request:
   - If requesting species support, attach a short summary and sample files.
   - If requesting assisted ingestion, attach a minimal circuit_config.json + morphology thumbnail and ask whether the team can ingest it as a project-private entity.
4. If the platform team confirms ingestion or fly support, upload your prepared artifacts into the project and then:
   - Open Workflows → Simulate → choose the simulation type (e.g., Small microcircuit beta),
   - Use the model picker (Project tab) to select your uploaded circuit,
   - Configure the campaign and Generate simulation(s) (the platform will create the four JSONs),
   - Launch simulations to execute compute.

---

## If you want, I can help with any of the following next steps
- Review your SONATA-style files (high-level checklist) and point out likely import issues.  
  - (I can list required fields and structural checks you should run before attempting to import.)
- Draft a concise Help/Feedback message you can submit to the platform to request fly species support or assisted ingestion.

calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.user: I want to simulate a neuron and see plateau potentials in the apical dendrite due to calcium. What ME model and simulation parameters should I use?
calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.expected_output: Select a hippocampal pyramidal ME-model. At present, the public rat and mouse hippocampus ME-Models have the brain region `Field CA1`, e-type `cACpyr` (for rat models and `cADpyr` for mouse models) and m-type `SP_PC`. Select one such pyramidal neuron model. I should include calcium ion channel mechanisms, then configure a simulation with current injection into distal apical dendrite section and set recording locations along the apical dendrite to capture voltage traces. Key parameters include setting the injection location to an apical section, using a sustained current stimulus protocol (like `idrest`), and enabling "record currents" to observe calcium ionic currents.
calcium_plateau_potentials_simulation.ai_response:
I'll look up the platform documentation for single-cell simulations and ME-model guidance.## Plan
- First, I'll give a concise set of recommended ME-model properties and **simulation parameters** that reliably produce / reveal calcium-mediated plateau potentials in apical dendrites.
- Then I’ll list practical protocols (current clamp vs clustered synaptic input), what to record, and useful manipulations to test the mechanism.
- If you want, I can next search the OBI catalogue for candidate ME-models (Layer‑5 pyramidal / models with apical tuft Ca channels) and return specific model IDs you can use.

---

## Which ME-model to use (model properties to require)
Pick an ME-model that explicitly contains:
- **Detailed apical dendritic morphology**, including a long apical trunk and tuft branches (apical tuft present).  
- **Calcium dynamics mechanisms** (intracellular Ca concentration state variable, calcium pumps/buffering) and explicit Ca-dependent currents.  
- **Voltage‑gated calcium channel (VGCC) mechanisms** present in apical dendrites — especially L‑type and/or R/T‑type and possibly high‑voltage activated channels that can support regenerative Ca currents.  
- **Sufficient spatial compartmentalization** (morphology split into compartments/sections that allow local depolarization in tuft to be sustained).  
- Optionally: **NMDA receptor synapses** (voltage-dependent Mg block) included on tuft spines/dendrites; useful for synaptically evoked plateaus.  
- If available: an ME-model that already has validated e‑types / m‑types for cortical pyramidal neurons (e.g., L5 pyramidal) — these are the most common cells showing apical plateau potentials.

If you want, I can search the platform for ME-models with \apical", 	uft", or \layer 5\ in their metadata and bring back candidate model IDs.

---

## Simulation parameters (recommended settings)
Use these settings as a baseline. You may need to tweak values depending on the specific model.

- **Duration**: 300–1500 ms (use 500–1000 ms for full plateau expression).  
- **Time step (dt)**: 0.025–0.1 ms. 0.025–0.05 ms is safer when calcium dynamics and fast spikes are both important.  
- **Temperature**: 32–36 °C (physiological temperatures increase channel kinetics and favor plateau generation).  
- **Initial membrane voltage (v_init)**: −70 to −80 mV depending on model; −75 mV is common.  
- **Extracellular calcium concentration**: 1.1 mM (default) to 2.0 mM — higher extracellular Ca increases release probability for synaptic protocols but can also alter Ca current driving force; adjust experimentally.  
- **Random seed**: fix for reproducibility (set seed = 1 or other fixed value).  
- **Spatial discretization**: use the model’s default if it’s properly meshed; if adjustable, ensure each dendritic section length ≤ 0.1 λ to preserve local non‑linearities.

---

## Stimulation protocols that evoke apical plateau potentials
Two robust approaches:

1. Clustered synaptic activation on the apical tuft (physiological / synaptic)
   - Place a cluster of 10–50 excitatory synapses (AMPA + NMDA) close together on a short tuft branch or on neighboring tuft branches (spine-like or dendritic segment).  
   - Activate them synchronously or in a short burst (e.g., 5–20 pulses at 50–200 Hz, or a single strong coincident volley).  
   - NMDA conductance and local depolarization relieve Mg2+ block and recruit VGCCs → regenerative plateau.  
   - Useful parameter ranges: AMPA rise/decay typical, NMDA time constant ~50–150 ms; scale synaptic weights until a local spike/plateau appears.

2. Local dendritic current injection (experimental / controlled)
   - Inject a long (50–300 ms) depolarizing current into a tuft/dendritic compartment or via a conductance clamp near the tuft (if the simulator supports dendritic injections).  
   - Use amplitude large enough to bring that dendritic segment to ~ −30 to −10 mV (typical range for recruiting Ca plateau), often tens to hundreds of pA depending on compartment size and model.  
   - Start with a ramped or slowly rising current to mimic synaptic summation; abrupt strong pulses can produce backpropagating APs instead of local plateaus.

Hybrid: combine a moderate current injection with clustered synaptic inputs to lower the required synaptic drive.

---

## Manipulations & checks to verify Ca-mediated plateau
- **Block VGCCs**: set calcium channel conductances to 0 — plateau should disappear if VGCCs are necessary.  
- **Block NMDA receptors**: remove NMDA component of synapses — if plateau is NMDA‑dependent, it will be reduced or abolished.  
- **Increase / decrease Ca buffering or extrusion**: slower extrusion or less buffering should broaden plateau (higher local [Ca2+] prolongs Ca-dependent currents).  
- **Localize manipulation**: change conductances only in tuft compartments to confirm a local mechanism.  
- **Record intracellular [Ca2+]** in the tuft — plateau should coincide with a large, sustained Ca transient.

---

## What to record (minimum)
- **Membrane voltage (Vm) at**:
  - Soma
  - Apical trunk (middle)
  - Apical tuft branch(s) near the stimulated site (at multiple distances from the site)
- **Intracellular [Ca2+]** in the tuft compartment(s) and trunk
- **Spike times** (somatic APs) to check if plateau triggers somatic bursts
- Optionally: local ionic currents (I_Ca, I_NMDA) in the stimulated tuft compartment

Sampling: record at dt ≤ 0.1 ms for Vm; for Ca you can use slightly larger sampling but keep it consistent with dynamics (e.g., 0.1–1 ms).

---

## Numerical & practical tips
- If plateaus fail to appear, try:
  - Increasing local VGCC conductance in tuft (multiply by 1.5–3x) — some morphologies underestimate dendritic VGCC density.  
  - Increasing the number of clustered synapses or synchrony (more simultaneous inputs).  
  - Using slower NMDA kinetics or increasing NMDA:AMPA ratio to favor sustained depolarization.  
- Watch for numerical instability if dt is too large or compartments too coarse; reduce dt or refine morphology discretization if voltages look noisy or runaways occur.
- Use a fixed random seed to compare conditions reproducibly.

---

## Example concrete configuration (starter)
- Model: L5 pyramidal ME-model with apical tuft and calcium dynamics (if available).  
- Initialization:
  - Duration: 800 ms  
  - dt: 0.025 ms  
  - Temperature: 34 °C  
  - v_init: −75 mV  
  - extracellular_calcium: 1.1 mM  
- Stimulus (synaptic clustered):
  - 30 excitatory synapses on a single tuft branch (AMPA + NMDA), all activated at t = 100 ms (or a 5‑pulse 100 Hz burst at t = 100 ms).  
  - AMPA weights tuned so a single synapse is subthreshold; cluster should summate to local depolarization ≈ −20 to −10 mV.  
- Record soma + multiple tuft sites + Ca concentration in tuft.

---

## Do you want me to find specific ME-models on the Open Brain Platform?
